book_claims.claim: Almost undetectable effect on blood glucose
book_claims.mechanism: Very low carbohydrate content and high fibre means virtually no glucose load

supplementary_research.finding: Increased green vegetable intake associated with 14% reduced risk of type 2 diabetes per 1.15 servings/day. Attributed to low glycemic load, high fiber, and magnesium content.
Meta-analysis of 95 studies: 10 servings/day of fruits and vegetables associated with 31% reduction in premature death. Green vegetables showed the strongest dose-response relationship.

Higher cruciferous and green leafy vegetable intake specifically associated with reduced subclinical atherosclerosis (carotid intima-media thickness) in older women. Effect not seen with other vegetable types.

culinary_pairings.source: Standard dressing for all green vegetables; enhances fat-soluble nutrient absorption
Universal aromatic for sauteed greens across all cuisines
Brightens flavors and enhances non-heme iron absorption
Classic pairing in stir-fries; complementary plant protein
Aromatics cut green bitterness; anti-inflammatory synergy
Omega-3 fats enhance carotenoid absorption; textural contrast
Adds umami and B-vitamins to greens

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber. Feeds beneficial gut bacteria producing short-chain fatty acids.
Chlorophyll contains magnesium at its center. Bioavailability moderate; cooking may release more from plant matrix.
Rapidly destroyed by heat and prolonged storage. Eat raw or lightly cooked for maximum retention.
Chlorophyll and its derivatives may have anti-mutagenic and antioxidant properties. Research is preliminary.
